name: M.S. in Computer Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 45 referenced courses do contain a genuine computer-engineering spine - CS 505/506/610 bridging foundations, then architecture (ECE 690), embedded systems (ECE 692), microprocessors (ECE 684), VLSI (ECE 658 to ECE 758), networking (ECE 636/637/681/683) and ML (ECE 744, ECE 754) - but with requirementGroups 0 the page presents them as an undifferentiated elective menu that also mixes in a large block of power-systems courses (ECE 610-619) more native to EE. Progression exists only in local pockets (ECE 601 feeds ECE 640/660; ECE 660 to ECE 661; ECE 658 to ECE 758).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a required-core plus concentration structure (e.g., a CompE core of architecture/embedded/VLSI plus tracked electives) so students see a coherent path rather than the full ECE course list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 2 (e.g., ECE 601 to ECE 660 to ECE 661), which is shallow but normal for a graduate electives menu, and missingCourseCount is only 3 (ECE 231, ECE 353, ECE 395) - all undergraduate ECE codes cited as background rather than broken graduate links. The main defect is data hygiene: ECE 613's prerequisiteRaw has its entire course description bled into the prerequisite field.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean prerequisite strings such as ECE 613 so they list only course codes, and mark ECE 231/353/395 explicitly as undergraduate-preparation references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the data, so a prospective student cannot see what competencies an M.S. in Computer Engineering certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 program learning outcomes (e.g., ability to design and evaluate computer architectures, embedded/VLSI systems, and networked systems) and map them to the core courses.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta cannot be computed (requirementGroups 0), so neither the standard 30-credit M.S. load nor the split between coursework, project (ECE 700B), and thesis (ECE 701B) is visible on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and show the credit distribution across required core, electives, and the project/thesis option.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific for most of the 37 sampled courses, but a few entries are blank (ECE 613, ECE 641) and there is no plan of study, no labeled terms, and no stated total, forcing the reader to infer structure from the course list.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study table and fill the missing descriptions for ECE 613 and ECE 641.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The elective set is notably current: 5G communications (ECE 642/644/645), cloud and IoT networking/security (ECE 683), high-performance network functions and data-center virtualization (ECE 681), smart-grid sensing (ECE 619), and two data-era courses in optimization for data engineering (ECE 744) and statistical machine learning (ECE 754).
dimensions.suggestion: Elevate the strongest modern electives (ECE 681/683/744/754) into a named 'systems and ML for computer engineering' track so their currency is visible up front.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are generally substantive and specific (e.g., ECE 690 on pipelined/superscalar and parallel architectures, ECE 692 on the embedded design process), but they are stored as truncated excerpts and at least two sampled courses (ECE 613, ECE 641) carry empty description text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Many graduate electives correctly carry null prerequisites, and where prerequisites exist they are usually clean course codes (ECE 660, ECE 658); clarity is reduced by ECE 613's prerequisite field absorbing description prose and by three undergraduate references (ECE 231/353/395) that read as dangling.

missingTopics.rationale: With deep architecture (ECE 690) and VLSI (ECE 658/758) courses but no security-of-hardware content, the program omits side-channel, secure-enclave, and hardware-root-of-trust topics now central to computer engineering.

ECE 690 covers classical parallel architectures but there is no dedicated course on GPU programming, domain-specific accelerators, or CUDA/heterogeneous systems that dominate current hardware practice.
ECE 754 teaches statistical ML in the abstract, yet no course connects it to accelerator design or on-device/edge inference, a defining computer-engineering specialization.
The graduate list has architecture and embedded courses but no OS/systems-software course, leaving a gap between CS 610 data structures and ECE 690 architecture.
VLSI design (ECE 658/758) is present without a companion verification/formal-methods course, despite verification consuming most modern chip-design effort.

emergingTrends.rationale: Purpose-built silicon for deep learning is the fastest-growing area of computer engineering; the ECE 690/658 sequence should add accelerator-architecture content.
Post-Moore scaling is shifting to chiplet-based, domain-specific designs that the current VLSI and architecture courses do not yet address.
Embedded computing (ECE 692) is well positioned to incorporate on-device ML inference, a major direction for IoT and embedded products.
Cloud/IoT security (ECE 683) could be extended toward trusted execution and confidential computing as hardware-enforced security becomes standard.
A research-oriented CompE M.S. should expose students to quantum-computing hardware and control as an emerging architectural frontier.

topSuggestions: Replace the flat elective menu with a required computer-engineering core plus named tracks (architecture/VLSI, embedded, networked systems, ML systems).
Publish program learning outcomes and the total credit requirement (with the project vs. thesis split) to close the transparency gaps left by null statedTotalCredits and requirementGroups 0.
Add a hardware-security course and a GPU/accelerator (heterogeneous-computing) course to cover the two largest modern gaps.
Introduce an ML-hardware/edge-AI course that links ECE 754 to accelerator and embedded design.
Fix data-quality issues: clean ECE 613's prerequisite string and fill the blank ECE 613/641 descriptions.
